Home / Defense / LMT
LMT

Lockheed's $186B Secret Nobody's Talking About

With a 416-jet F-35 pipeline and the West's only operational hypersonic weapon, $LMT trades at 16x forward earnings while the world rearms.

Jun 21, 2026 1 min read

$186 billion. That's Lockheed Martin's backlog — revenue locked in before breakfast. While the market obsesses over AI, $LMT trades at 16x forward earnings. And here's the kicker: Lockheed is the only defense prime with a fifth-gen fighter in full-rate production and a fielded hypersonic weapon. Not one or the other. Both.

$117.8BMarket Cap
$186.4BBacklog
15.9xForward P/E
$75.1BRevenue (TTM)

The F-35 Pipeline

Lockheed delivered a record 191 F-35s in 2025 with a 416-aircraft backlog. Lots 18 and 19 — $24.3 billion for 296 jets — are funded and rolling. Every NATO ally is buying in, and sustainment just booked another $2.29 billion.

▶ Lockheed Martin Ramps Production
Fox Business — The Signal

Hypersonics Are Here

Hypersonics are the growth story nobody's priced in. The Dark Eagle deploys its first operational battery in 2026, backed by $2.7 billion. It flies Mach 5+, maneuvers mid-flight, and makes existing missile defense obsolete. The Navy wants Conventional Prompt Strike — $1.36 billion already booked. Lockheed is the only Western contractor with an operational hypersonic program. Full stop.

Missiles & Fire Control is up 8% year-over-year, fueled by a $9.8 billion Patriot PAC-3 contract and quadrupling THAAD production. This isn't a spike — it's structural.

The Bottom Line

Here's the math: $75 billion in trailing revenue, a $186 billion backlog still growing, and the stock at 15.9x forward earnings — cheaper than the S&P 500 — with a 2.70% dividend yield and a near-zero beta. Lockheed printed $1.49 billion in net earnings in Q1 2026, with full-year free cash flow guided to $6.5-$6.8 billion. The Q1 FCF dip was purely timing.

Global defense spending just hit an all-time high. The U.S. FY2026 defense budget is north of $900 billion. Every major ally is increasing procurement. The F-35 backlog alone guarantees a decade of production, and hypersonics are just beginning to scale. You're paying 16x earnings for 123,000 employees, a moat measured in decades of classified IP, and customers — the U.S. government and its allies — that aren't going anywhere. When software companies trade at 30x revenue with no profits, Lockheed at 16x earnings feels like someone left the price tag wrong.

— The Signal
